museum.name: Gaming Time Machine
museum.dek: The year closes with Space Invaders as the unmistakable headline, Atari Football as a physical-control landmark, and Namco's Gee Bee as a quiet origin point.
museum.curatorNote: December is an end-of-year synthesis rather than a fake December release list.

sections.title: News
sections.subtitle: Five researched moments from an early videogame year, with broad context separated from confident month-level claims.

items.title: Space Invaders is now the year's defining artefact
items.body: The cabinet has changed the tone of arcades and created a template for the coming years.
items.sourceNote: Space Invaders 1978 context.

items.title: The trackball has a public identity
items.body: Atari Football makes an input device feel like part of the sport.
items.sourceNote: Atari Football October 1978 context.

items.title: Namco has entered original arcade development
items.body: Gee Bee is a modest beginning for a company that will shape the next arcade decade.
items.sourceNote: Gee Bee context.

items.title: Home consoles still feel behind the arcade
items.body: The living-room machine cannot yet deliver the Space Invaders experience most players want.
items.sourceNote: Space Invaders VCS arrives in 1980.

items.title: UK players carry the sound home in memory
items.body: The arcade is still public, loud and local: the kind of place you visit, not a site you browse.
items.sourceNote: Period arcade culture framing.

sections.title: Releases
sections.subtitle: A fuller shelf, mixing confirmed month anchors with clearly labelled year-level context.

items.title: Space Invaders
items.body: Taito's fixed shooter becomes the gravity well of the arcade business. Sources differ on whether to foreground April publication or June Japanese release.
items.certainty: verified 1978; month depends on source definition
items.sourceNote: Space Invaders sources cite April publication and June Japan release; North American timing is also staggered.

items.title: Gee Bee
items.body: Namco's first original arcade game, a pinball-and-Breakout hybrid by Toru Iwatani before Pac-Man.
items.sourceNote: Gee Bee sources place Namco's release in October 1978.

items.title: Atari Football
items.body: A trackball-driven American football cabinet that makes physical control part of the spectacle.
items.certainty: verified October in Arcade-History and MobyGames
items.sourceNote: Arcade-History and MobyGames list October 1978.

items.title: Fire Truck
items.body: Atari's cooperative driving game, with one player steering the cab and another the trailer.
items.sourceNote: Fire Truck sources place it in 1978.

items.title: Avalanche
items.body: Atari's falling-rocks action game, a clean example of late-1970s abstract cabinet design.
items.sourceNote: Avalanche sources place it in 1978.

items.title: World Cup / soccer trackball context
items.body: Taito/Sega football cabinet context matters because trackball sports controls were developing before Atari Football popularised them in the US.
items.sourceNote: Football histories note earlier Taito/Sega trackball soccer context before Atari Football.

items.title: Atari Video Pinball
items.body: A useful transitional object between mechanical pinball, dedicated TV games and later digital pinball.
items.sourceNote: Video Pinball sources describe Atari's dedicated console line beginning in 1977 and arcade/video context around 1978-79.

items.title: Space Invaders clones and imitators
items.body: Not a single release so much as a cultural aftershock: once Space Invaders exists, the industry starts orbiting it.
items.sourceNote: Included as industry context, not a named product release.

sections.title: Hardware
sections.subtitle: Four objects explaining the month: public cabinets, home consoles, microcomputers and control technology.

items.title: Space Invaders arcade board
items.body: By December 1978, Taito's cabinet represents the new centre of arcade gravity, even where local rollout is still uneven.
items.sourceNote: Space Invaders hardware and release histories.

items.title: Atari VCS before its killer app
items.body: The cartridge console exists, but it has not yet received the arcade conversion that will transform its sales.
items.sourceNote: Atari VCS and Space Invaders VCS context.

items.title: Trackball control
items.body: Atari Football helps make the trackball a memorable public control surface.
items.sourceNote: Atari Football sources.

items.title: Microcomputer magazine culture
items.body: Home computer games still travel through listings, ads and specialist computing magazines more than through ordinary game retail.
items.sourceNote: Magazine archive context.

sections.title: Online Life
sections.subtitle: Networked play exists at the edges, but ordinary players live in a world of paper, cabinets and local knowledge.

items.title: Online life was not the ordinary visitor experience
items.body: For most players in December 1978, games are discovered through arcades, shops, magazines, clubs and word of mouth rather than online services.
items.sourceNote: Included to avoid projecting later online habits backward.

items.title: Institutional networks remain the edge case
items.body: PLATO, university systems and early networked computing matter historically, but they are not normal UK consumer gaming.
items.sourceNote: PLATO and early networked game context.

items.title: Paper is still the search engine
items.body: Magazines, adverts, catalogues, manuals and club newsletters explain what machines and games exist.
items.sourceNote: BYTE, Creative Computing, PCW and electronics-magazine context.

items.title: The arcade is the live feed
items.body: High scores, queues and overheard advice make the cabinet a public information system.
items.sourceNote: Period arcade culture context.

sections.title: What It Felt Like
sections.subtitle: A young medium, a patchy record, and a future split between public machines and home hardware.

items.title: The future was still public
items.body: The arcade suddenly feels invaded: one cabinet can pull a room into a shared rhythm of shots, shields and descending pressure.

items.title: The home shelf was changing shape
items.body: Home games still feel smaller than the public machine. The VCS is promising, but the arcade owns the spectacle.

items.title: Britain saw the edges first
items.body: In Britain, this is a public memory first: cafes, arcades, seaside amusements, coins and the sound of aliens advancing.

items.title: The record is still uneven
items.body: Exact dates are often regional, trade-led or retrospective. The exhibit keeps month-level certainty separate from year-level context.

editorNotes: Initial AI-generated research draft for 1978. Sparse months intentionally avoid invented releases.
Magazine cover images are reconstructed placeholders and should be replaced with verified/licensed scans if used editorially.
Several arcade, console and computer-game entries are year-level or source-conflicted; sourceNote fields mark uncertainty.
